text:
Herein, we disclose a highly enantioselective radical hydroamination of enol esters with sulfonamides by means of a complementary iridium photocatalyst/Bronsted acid/tetrapeptide thiol catalyst. By using this combination of catalysts, the targeted $\beta$-amino ester products—obtained in good to excellent yields for 23 examples—can be obtained with enhanced enantiocontrol up to 97:3. Critically, this catalytic system achieves total control of the crucial hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) stereochemistry, involving a chiral cysteine containing peptide to hybridize and subsequently reduce the prochiral carbon-centred radical species involved in the key hydroamination event.

A detailed structural optimization of the peptide substrate and alkene reactivity was carried out to establish a basis for design of catalysts with optimal performance, based on the roles of the $i+2$ and $i+3$ amino acids. Kinetic studies and Hammett analysis, supported by density functional theory calculations, showed that the stereochemistry of the reaction is controlled by a complex interplay of non-covalent interactions including inter- and intramolecular hydrogen bonds, $\pi$-$\pi$ stacking and London dispersion forces that direct the radical species in the binding site of the peptide catalyst. In all, this work significantly advances the field of catalytic asymmetric hydroaromatic transfer (HAT) and provides detailed insight into the modular peptide approach to controlling the reactivity of open-shell intermediates via non-covalent binding.
